author: Dr. Jessica Tanaka
content:
RHEUMATOLOGY NEW PATIENT CONSULTATION — 4/10/2026, 1:30 PM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
DOB: 8/12/2003 (Age 22)
Referring source: Dr. Manuel Reyes, Patterson Park Urgent Care (1/12/2026)
Interpreter: Spanish-language phone interpreter, Language Line.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
New-patient rheumatology evaluation for symmetric polyarthritis and constitutional symptoms with positive ANA.

INTERVAL HISTORY (since urgent care 1/12/2026 and 1/26/2026 follow-up):
Ms. Sandoval is now ~8 months from symptom onset, ~3 months on levothyroxine 25 mcg daily. Disease-modifying therapy was deferred at the urgent care visits pending today's evaluation. She reports the following with mother and Spanish interpreter present:

- Joint pain: Persistent — she has not noticed meaningful change since onset. Morning stiffness remains >60 minutes. The MCPs and PIPs of both hands and her wrists remain the most affected joints. She has had occasional brief flares with ankle and knee involvement after long shifts at work.
- Fatigue: Persistent, possibly slightly worse over the past month. She works 25-30 hours/week in housekeeping and feels she 'cannot do as much as before.'
- Hair loss: Diffuse shedding continues; she now ties her hair back to hide the thinning. No patchy alopecia, no scalp tenderness, no scaling.
- Skin: She mentions a faint, mildly pink area across her cheeks that has been intermittent over the last 2 months, more noticeable after sun exposure. Denies discoid lesions, ulcers, livedo. No new rash.
- Constitutional: Reports unintentional weight loss of approximately 3-4 lbs over the past 3 months (clothing fits looser). No fevers, no night sweats currently. Appetite somewhat decreased — partly attributed to stress.
- Menses: Remain irregular; last period 6 weeks ago.
- Cardiopulmonary: No chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, Raynaud, sicca symptoms.
- GI: No dysphagia, no abdominal pain, no bowel changes.
- Neuropsych: No headaches, focal weakness, seizures. Reports mild depressed mood related to financial and immigration stressors but no SI.

ADHERENCE: Levothyroxine sometimes missed when she is rushed in the morning, but estimates >80% adherence.

INSURANCE / ACCESS: Esperanza Health PCP intake completed 2/10/2026 (Dr. Andre Williams, FQHC). Mother is working with patient on Medicaid reinstatement; application submitted ~6 weeks ago, no decision yet. Today's rheumatology visit funded by Hopkins financial-aid program.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY, ALLERGIES, MEDICATIONS, FAMILY HISTORY, SOCIAL HISTORY: As documented previously.

LABS REVIEWED:
From 1/12/2026 (urgent care): ANA 1:80 homogeneous; RF negative; TSH 5.32; CBC and BMP unremarkable; UA bland.
From 1/26/2026 send-outs (already in chart): anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-Ro/La, anti-CCP all negative; C3 and C4 normal; hep B/C and HIV negative; parvovirus IgM negative; TPO antibody positive; free T4 normal; UA/PCR normal.
Labs drawn today (preliminary point-of-care + send-outs):
- TSH 3.18 (normalized on levothyroxine), free T4 1.2 (normal)
- ESR 22 mm/hr (mildly elevated; ref <20)
- CRP 0.6 mg/dL (mildly elevated; ref <0.5)
- Repeat ANA, dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-Ro/La, anti-U1-RNP, anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70, anti-Jo-1 — pending send-out (Quest)
- Repeat C3, C4, CH50 — pending
- Vitamin D 25-OH, vitamin B12, folate, ferritin — pending

IMAGING REVIEWED:
2018 CXR (immigration screening) — normal. No other imaging on file. Hand X-rays ordered today (see below).

VITALS: BP 110/68, HR 84, RR 14, T 98.2 F, SpO2 100% RA. Weight 48.8 kg (down ~2 kg from January). Height 150 cm. BMI 21.7.

FOCUSED RHEUMATOLOGIC EXAM:
General: Thin young woman, appears slightly tired but in no distress. Cooperative through interpreter. Slight cachetic appearance noted.
Skin: Faint, ill-defined erythema across cheeks bilaterally — not classic malar but suggestive; sparing nasolabial folds. No discoid lesions. No oral ulcers (specifically inspected hard and soft palate, buccal mucosa). No livedo. No nail-fold capillary abnormalities to gross inspection. Diffuse non-scarring scalp hair thinning.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
CV: RRR, normal S1/S2, no rub, no murmur.
Lungs: Clear bilaterally.
MSK: Symmetric synovitis of MCP 2-5 and PIP 2-4 of both hands with tenderness and mild dorsal soft-tissue swelling. Wrists tender, mildly swollen, with limited dorsiflexion. Elbow exam unremarkable. Shoulders, hips, knees with intact range of motion, no effusions. Ankles with minimal synovial thickening, tender talocrural. Squeeze test of MCPs positive bilaterally; MTP squeeze negative. No deformities. No proximal muscle weakness on confrontational testing (5/5 throughout). No tender points consistent with fibromyalgia.
Neuro: Grossly intact.

ASSESSMENT:

Ms. Sandoval is a 22-year-old woman with approximately 9 months of progressive symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis (predominantly small joints of the hands and wrists with associated ankle involvement), constitutional fatigue, diffuse non-scarring alopecia, possible mild facial erythema, modest weight loss, low-titer positive ANA (1:80 homogeneous), normal complements, negative specific lupus and RA serologies, mildly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 22, CRP 0.6), and concurrent autoimmune thyroiditis (TPO Ab positive, TSH now normalized on levothyroxine).

Clinically she does not yet meet 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria for SLE (entry requirement of ANA ≥1:80 met, but additive criteria score is limited — possible mucocutaneous involvement is the only soft additional finding). However, the clinical picture — particularly the persistent symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis, alopecia, and family history of autoimmune disease — keeps SLE high on the differential, and early SLE can present with a paucity of specific autoantibodies and only later evolve.

My working impression is undifferentiated connective tissue disease, with early SLE remaining the leading specific consideration. The Hashimoto's thyroiditis is a co-existing autoimmune diagnosis (organ-specific) which raises the prior probability for a systemic autoimmune disorder.

I have explicitly discussed with the patient and her mother (through interpreter) that:
- We do not have a definitive diagnosis yet.
- We will start hydroxychloroquine today, which is appropriate for this clinical picture and may delay progression of SLE if that is what this becomes.
- We will add a short course of low-dose prednisone for symptomatic control of her joint inflammation.
- We need close follow-up and re-assessment in 3-4 months for evolution of the clinical phenotype and repeat serologies.
- She must have baseline ophthalmology for HCQ — I provided a list of low-cost options.

There is significant concern about her access to ongoing specialty care. The patient is uninsured and the financial-aid coverage for today is one-time. I have asked the Hopkins social work team to engage with her to advocate for Medicaid reinstatement.

PLAN:
1. Start hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO BID. Provided 90-day supply through patient-assistance program. Counseled re: ocular toxicity, need for baseline ophthalmology before starting.
2. Start prednisone 10 mg PO daily x 4 weeks then taper by 2.5 mg every week — total ~7 weeks. Counseled about side effects, signs of infection, glucose monitoring; provided written Spanish instructions.
3. Continue levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily.
4. Discontinue chronic ibuprofen given low-dose steroid initiation; PRN only for breakthrough.
5. Labs ordered today: comprehensive repeat panel (see above) plus CBC, CMP, urinalysis with protein:creatinine, vitamin levels, ferritin.
6. Hand X-rays — bilateral (PA, oblique) — ordered to assess for erosive change.
7. Ophthalmology — referral renewed; financial-aid pathway initiated; baseline exam needed within next month.
8. Bone density not indicated at this time given short steroid course; calcium and vitamin D supplementation recommended.
9. Social work consult arranged for insurance navigation and prescription assistance.
10. Patient education materials on UCTD/SLE provided in Spanish.
11. Follow-up: rheumatology in 3 months (target 7/10/2026), or sooner if new symptoms (rash, fever, oral ulcers, chest pain, dyspnea, swelling, dark/foamy urine).

Visit time: 75 minutes (interpreter time 42 minutes).
attestation:
Electronically signed by: Dr. Jessica Tanaka, MD
Rheumatology, Johns Hopkins
Date: 4/10/2026
